data.title: Spyre Therapeutics: Strong SPY001 Data Set Up A Catalyst-Rich 2026
data.description:
Thesis

Spyre Therapeutics (SYRE) has just delivered some pretty strong early signals regarding SPY001. So far, it's shown a 9.2-point RHI reduction with about 40% clinical remission and 51% endoscopic improvement at just 12 weeks. These are results that would suggest a lot faster and deeper efficacy than legacy α4β7 therapies like vedolizumab, hence the bullish market reaction. But keep in mind that vedolizumab trials had different populations, designs, and endpoints, so we cannot make a head-to-head comparison yet. It also managed to maintain a relatively clean safety profile in ulcerative colitis.

As you can probably tell, the implications here are quite meaningful in that SPY001 has the potential to come out as a best-in-class, gut-selective backbone therapy. We also have the added upside from combination regimens to factor in, as I'll explain. The latter here could further enhance efficacy with reduced systemic immunosuppression risk, which would be an upside-down case scenario going forward. I think the market is trying to price in the potential of this platform evolving into a multi-asset IBD franchise with blockbuster potential and platform-level value creation here. So my buy case would then be that the stock is a forward-looking bet on SPY001 and its combination strategy, which we could see redefine the treatment paradigm in ulcerative colitis.

SPY001

As you know, Spyre has reported some pretty strong interim data readout from their Phase 2 SKYLINE trial. It's surrounding their lead asset, SPY001, and would seem to set the candidate up as a potential best-in-class therapy for ulcerative colitis. Just for a bit of background, SPY001 is a monoclonal antibody specifically designed to target the α4β7 integrin. Now, that integrin is a key molecule we usually see that directs immune cells, in particular lymphocytes, to migrate into gut tissue where they drive inflammation. So, mechanistically, here things seem quite simple, in theory: by blocking α4β7 from binding to its ligand on gut endothelial cells, SPY001 can then selectively prevent those inflammatory immune cells from entering the intestinal lining.

The approach would make the treatment gut-selective, and it's actually a mechanism that's already validated by vedolizumab. However, the difference here is that SPY001 is engineered with an extended half-life and optimised dosing with the goal of increasing the drug's exposure and durability. In practice, that would mean it's administered as an injectable biologic, but still has the goal of less frequent dosing and stronger early disease control compared to a lot of existing therapies on the market.

Now, prior to this readout, α4β7 inhibitors like vedolizumab had already demonstrated a pretty solid safety profile with moderate efficacy. But the main criticism was that it came with a much slower onset and, a lot of the time, incomplete remission rates. So it's clear what Spyre is trying to do here in terms of increasing target coverage, specifically by using higher and more sustained antibody levels during induction. It would make the main differentiation here deeper and faster responses for SPY001.

SKYLINE trial

As for the SKYLINE trial, this was the goal. It's a multi-arm, platform Phase 2 study evaluating three antibodies in SPY001, SPY002, and SPY003; all were evaluated alone and in combination. For part A, which is the portion that just got reported, it's an open-label induction study in which each drug is tested individually at a single dose level to just quickly assess the signal. Part B then transitions into a randomised, placebo-controlled design with multiple dose levels and combination regimens. Now, by doing this kind of structure, it actually allows for rapid early readouts while still preserving rigour in later stages, as we just saw.

So, the 12-week Part A results for SPY001 were notably strong for this class, hence the market reaction. The drug managed to achieve its primary endpoint with a highly statistically significant 9.2-point reduction in the Robarts Histopathology Index (RHI). That measure is a pretty stringent measure of microscopic inflammation, so it would suggest not just symptom relief here but actual mucosal healing at the tissue level, which was good to see. The secondary endpoints also made for good viewing. About 40% of patients achieved clinical remission, based on a modified Mayo score, and about 51% showed endoscopic improvement. And if we put these figures in context with the historical benchmarks for existing biologics in similar populations, they would appear to be quite favourable.

As for safety, this remained pretty clean and consistent with the α4β7 class. Only about 14% of patients experienced adverse events, with no drug-related serious events, no discontinuations, and, importantly, no deaths. So we have a combination of high efficacy and low systemic toxicity, which I think is pretty telling of a gut-selective mechanism. A big aim would be to avoid broader immune suppression, and the data so far would suggest that's the case.

SPY001 is being pitched as a potential best-in-class narrative here, which certainly adds to the stock momentum, but on some level, I think it's justified. You see, we have the convergence of three factors so far, which I like the look of: magnitude of efficacy, speed, and depth of response, and also the pharmacologic convenience. SPY001 would also seem to outperform prior α4β7 therapies on some key clinical and endoscopic endpoints while also maintaining their hallmark safety advantage. There's a pretty decent extended half-life that could also translate into less frequent dosing. Less frequent dosing would then imply improved patient adherence and quality of life.

We also have to consider the framework in which Spyre is setting up SPY001. They clearly don't want it viewed as a standalone therapy but rather as a backbone for combination regimens with cytokine-targeting antibodies such as SPY002, which can target TL1A, or even SPY003, which can target IL-23. So there is a potential upside case scenario for Spyre in which we see further enhanced efficacy without compounding systemic risk. That would make up Part B, which is now enrolling, and we can expect combination data over the next 1 to 2 years. Now, this seems like a very long timeframe, but the program is still very much evolving here. The end goal would be more of forming a strong monotherapy story, which can become a broader platform strategy to reshape treatment paradigms in inflammatory bowel disease.

Valuation: sensitive to upcoming Phase 2/3 data

From a valuation standpoint, Spyre is obviously in the pre-revenue, clinical-stage biotech category. So it would be best to value the company on its balance sheet strength and pipeline potential here. They hold a market cap of about $4.0 billion and roughly $756 million in cash prior to the public offering they announced yesterday. It gives them an enterprise value closer to $3.4 billion, so effectively, that's what the market is paying for its drug portfolio, led by SPY001. One of the few usable relative metrics would be price-to-book, which screens as pretty expensive. It sits in the 7x to 8x range and is significantly above the healthcare sector median, which is more near the 2.6x to 3.0x range. So that tells me there's a substantial premium for future innovation rather than current assets.

I think what the market is implicitly pricing in would be a decent probability of clinical and commercial success across not just one but multiple programs. So not just SPY001 as a monotherapy, but also its role in combination regimens. That $3 to 4 billion enterprise value at Phase 2 would also imply investors are underwriting a scenario here in which SPY001 can meaningfully compete with or even exceed existing standards like vedolizumab in ulcerative colitis. So it does set a lot of expectations for the company. We have a potential blockbuster status of about $1 billion plus in peak sales and also a lot of optionality around the broader platform.

Over the next few years, the picture will become a lot clearer around SPY002 and SPY003 combinations, which could expand into a multi-asset franchise in inflammatory bowel disease in the best upside case. However, there's a lack of revenue and negative forward EPS growth expectations to come with that early clinical stage success, so it means a lot of this valuation is speculative and highly sensitive to upcoming Phase 2/3 data. Overall, I feel the stock is trading less on fundamentals and more as a forward-looking bet on best-in-class efficacy with a nice differentiated combination strategy.

A word on the balance sheet

Post that positive readout, the stock closed in the mid-$63 range yesterday, and as some expected, Spyre announced a public equity offering. The stock was in a strong position, and the raise is tipped to be about $300 million with an additional $45 million greenshoe option. It was a typical biotech move to capitalise on the strong clinical momentum and fund ongoing development, which is very much needed with the upcoming Phase 2/3 expansion of SPY001 and combination programs.

Now, if we assume the base raise closes, the pro forma cash would increase from $756 million to about $1.05 billion and up to $1.10 billion if the full greenshoe is exercised. If we take the FY25 operating spend of about $210 million as a proxy for burn here, Spyre's new pro forma cash position would then give us a runway of about 5 years. That would extend funding comfortably into 2030 to 2031 if we assume similar spending levels. However, I think we can assume spending will likely increase as trials scale.

Looking ahead

So to sum up, Spyre seems to be entering a pretty catalyst-rich phase where execution over the next 12 to 24 months is extremely critical for the stock. We have about six readouts expected across 2026, all of which should serve as potential stock catalysts, including SPY002 mid-year, SPY003 sometime in 3Q26, and SPY072 in rheumatic diseases by year-end. The company is also pretty well set up for a sustained data flow that could drive multiple re-rating events if successful here. Importantly, we should consider that the strong early signal from SPY001 does a lot more than just de-risk a single asset, but rather it supports the upside case in that it optimises validated mechanisms via higher exposure and longer half-life. This is the key to unlocking superior efficacy, and combining these potential best-in-class components in a bull-case scenario would push outcomes beyond the current therapeutic ceiling in inflammatory bowel disease. Ultimately, I see the story as now shifting from promise to proof in that if upcoming datasets consistently reinforce SPY001's profile that we just saw and also show us some additive benefit from combinations, Spyre could come out of 2026 not just with a competitive drug, but with a differentiated platform.

data.title: My Top Defensive Picks For An Uncertain 2026
data.description:
Co-authored with Beyond Saving.

The major market indices have been giving up ground, and the Fear & Greed Index has been in the "Extreme Fear" zone for quite some time, only easing a bit with yesterday's developments in the Middle East. Source.

The headlines are focused on soaring oil prices (CO1:COM) and the evolving war in Iran. Yet, considering the reality of the situation, the major market indices have been rather tame. They are down, but it's hardly dramatic.

The conflict in Iran is creating stress on the global economy, primarily due to the limited traffic through the Strait of Hormuz. We discussed with members how this has ramifications throughout the world, including the U.S. economy.

For now, despite a ceasefire being in effect and negotiations underway, there is an ongoing threat of hostilities resuming and causing a reversal of the current rally, which is an obvious potential catalyst.

So what does that mean for investors? Should you just sell everything and sit on the sidelines? Should you run outside with your hair on fire, screaming about the sky falling?

To borrow a Yogi-ism, forecasting is hard, especially about the future. If the Strait of Hormuz remains closed for months, it has enormous economic and humanitarian ramifications for the world. If it opens up tomorrow, that relieves a lot of the risk suddenly.

We don't want to go to cash because we use the cash flow from our portfolio, and selling comes with the risk that things turn out well and prices remain high. On the other hand, we want to avoid buying things that are trading at high valuations and are at risk of declining more. Buying low is always a good idea when you are investing, regardless of whether the future is bullish or bearish.

Above all, make sure you are buying investments that you are comfortable holding through a downswing. If you're going to bail and sell something because it is down 50%, then you probably shouldn't be buying it today.

Let's talk about what I'm buying.

Fixed-Income

Preferreds

I've been pounding the table on fixed-income for three years. The largest part of my fixed-income portfolio is preferred equity. Preferred equity provides a predetermined dividend that is senior to the common but junior to the debt. This means that in order to pay a common dividend, the company has to be current on its preferred dividends. However, it is equity, and it is subordinate to debt, which means that its credit risk is a bit higher.

The result is that investors can get a higher yield from preferred equity than from debt, while getting a predetermined and more stable dividend payment than you get from the common equity. Investors want to make sure that they are buying preferred shares with companies that are good credit risks.

Our strategy is heavily focused on preferred outside of banks and insurance companies. We don't have anything against them, but the preferred for banks and insurance companies tends to be a more crowded trade with fewer good deals. Preferred or baby bonds for REITs (Real Estate Investment Trusts), BDCs (Business Development Companies), CEFs (Closed-End Funds), and other RICs (Regulated Investment Companies) often have higher yields while taking on what we view as an acceptable credit risk.

Virtus InfraCap US Preferred Stock ETF (PFFA) is an ETF that focuses on preferred stocks and holds many of the preferred stocks we invest in at HDO. It has materially outperformed peer funds that follow the index and are heavily invested in bank and insurance preferred.

PFFA is a great investment option; however, it is an ETF. That means it will adjust its dividend to reflect its taxable income. In 2020, we saw preferred prices crash, and PFFA cut its distribution, even though most preferred continued paying their dividends. PFFA and other preferred funds will provide you with the economic return of preferred equities, but they will not necessarily provide you with the predictable recurring cash flow.

I own both. I hold PFFA for the economic return and the leveraged exposure to preferred equities. I also hold a large portfolio of individual preferred equities that pay me a predetermined dividend and will have more cash flow stability in the event of a crash like COVID-19.

If you aren't actively withdrawing and you don't plan on withdrawing cash, then PFFA, combined with some other funds for diversification, might be a more efficient way to have broad exposure to the asset class. However, if you are relying on income in your portfolio, then you definitely want to have significant holdings in fixed-income assets with a combination of preferred and bonds.

Bonds

Like preferred, bonds are fixed-income, but they pay interest, and they are a legal obligation. This means that if a borrower defaults, the bondholders can sue, and usually the company ends up going to bankruptcy court. As a result, bonds are usually of a lower credit risk.

But be aware, this relative safety can vary company to company. Some companies like Chimera Investment Corporation (CIM) have a very thin difference between their baby bonds and preferred. The majority of their debt is secured and senior to their baby bonds. As a result, in a bankruptcy situation, odds are high that the baby bonds and preferred would have a materially similar (and bad) recovery. We aren't buying CIM bonds because we believe that they would recover in bankruptcy; we buy them because we believe that bankruptcy is a remote risk for CIM. The attraction of the bonds over the preferred is the maturity date.

CIM's preferred are perpetual, which means that their yield will always reflect whatever the market believes is fair to reflect the current credit risk. Bonds mature on a specific date. As that date approaches, the bond's price will approach par unless bankruptcy is imminent because the bond will be redeemed at par. As a result, bonds that are near maturity will have prices that trend closer to par than bonds with more distant maturities. And bonds will have prices that are stickier than preferred. The bond will repay par at a known point in the future (barring bankruptcy), whereas the perpetual preferred can remain outstanding forever. So, there is no reason for it to "have to" return to par.

We recommend setting up a maturity ladder (a concept we have many times on Seeking Alpha), so that you have bonds that are maturing every year. This will provide your portfolio with a constant flow of maturities. The bonds closest to maturity will tend to stay closer to par, and in a crash, you will receive cash from maturities, or you can sell bonds that are close to maturity near par to take advantage of other buying opportunities.

Agency mREITs

We've discussed agency mREITs (mortgage REITs) as a countercyclical option that usually performs well during recessions. Annaly Capital Management (NLY) is the oldest mREIT to follow the agency MBS strategy. Here is how it did in 2000-2002:

And here is 2007-2009:

In both cases, the combination of an oil price spike and a faltering economy caused outsized returns for NLY.

The reason this happens is that NLY invests in agency MBS, an asset class that has minimal credit risk. The agencies, Fannie Mae and Freddie Mac buy mortgages and then put a guarantee on them. If the borrower defaults, the agency buys back the mortgage at par. As a result, agency MBS does well when investors are fleeing to safety.

This makes NLY and its peers a powerful option to protect your portfolio from a declining economy. It is during recessions that they are raising their dividends the most, and during bull markets when they are reducing their dividends.

Tangible Businesses

Infrastructure, REITs, MLPs (Master Limited Partnership), and other businesses that own tangible and essential infrastructure that generate cash flows, make up a huge portion of our stock picks. The reason is that these cash cows produce the dividends that achieve our portfolio goals and tend to have stable cash flow in a wide variety of economic conditions.

We are especially bullish on these today because we see valuations as relatively low for most "Value" stocks, and these are quintessential Value stocks. They are companies that are valued based on their current cash flow.

There's downside risk to these types of investments in terms of price, and there could be risk if their sector is the center of a recession. In 2008, REITs weren't the best option for cash flow stability. In 2001, they were. We don't believe that REITs, oil, or infrastructure are likely to be materially weak during the next recession, but we diversify because we don't know the future.

We believe that the current set-up most closely resembles the Dot-com Bust. Here is how one of our REIT funds, Cohen & Steers Quality Income Realty Fund (RQI), performed from 2002-2004:

Credit Risk

The portion of our portfolio that is currently underperforming the most is credit risk. While defaults objectively remain low and we haven't seen major credit losses in our picks, sentiment has definitely turned negative toward the types of credit holdings we have exposure to – especially CLO (Collateralized Loan Obligation) equity and BDCs.

We've discussed in other articles why we believe the negative sentiment is inaccurate and is overstating the risks, but it has a real and meaningful impact on share prices. In the near-term, we expect sentiment to remain negative. If there is a positive shift in the overall market from fear to greed, say the Iranian war ends with a successful peace deal, and the markets turn euphoric, we could see a "risk on" move, and these holdings would experience a rapid recovery.

Otherwise, it will come down to the numbers. The actual default rate will be X%, and to the extent that it is less than the market expects, it will be positive for prices and dividends. And if it is more than the market is currently expecting, that will be negative for prices and dividends.

Valuations are very low for some credit risk options, and it's an interesting opportunity to add if you have already addressed the stability and safety of your portfolio with the holdings discussed above.

Conclusion

We can't predict the future, but we can construct a portfolio that is ready for it. The portfolio discussed above is one that is biased toward safety if there is a recession. It is heavy in fixed-income, has a maturity ladder, has countercyclical holdings, and investments in cash cows. Those are all investments that we would expect to outperform if a bear market occurs. They might not be immune to price swings, but they will produce ample dividends, providing us with cash for as long as the bear market persists.

Once that base is in place, we view credit risk as a positive risk/reward. By that, we mean higher risk, but a very high potential reward, making the risk worthwhile. Credit risk investments is where we could see very large returns if the market's current bearishness towards CLOs and BDCs wanes.

Put together, we have a portfolio that we believe is optimized to face the challenging investment environment we are in today.

data.title: Cisco Remains A Buy As AI Tailwinds Broaden
data.description:
As you can see in the chart below, I have changed my view on Cisco Systems, Inc. (CSCO) quite a few times in the past year or so. Nonetheless, in my most recent article, I upgraded the stock to a buy rating citing AI infrastructure momentum acceleration and undervaluation relative to the IT sector. Since then, there has been some mild gains which isn't bad considering the risk-off environment recently. While the company reported earnings quite a while back, their next batch of results aren't due until mid next month and so today I have decided to provide a fresh perspective and an update for the investment thesis.

Below, it is shown that Cisco's fundamentals are quite solid despite some mixed numbers. Top line growth accelerated and operating margin held largely steady. Looking forward, guidance is also encouraging and despite some near term risks like the Middle East conflict, AI tailwinds are broadening for the company. Their expertise in wireless connectivity and cybersecurity are set to pay off. With the forward P/E still at a discount to the sector, a reiteration of my buy rating is justified in my view.

Growth Looks Solid

We'll start today with a look at their FY2026 Q2 top line numbers. In the quarter, the company as a whole generated revenues of $15.35 billion, up 9.71% YoY. While this isn't the most exciting of growth rates, you can see above that this was an acceleration from the previous quarter and that relative to the past few years, Cisco is performing in a reasonably strong manner right now. The company also managed to breeze past expectations as they beat by over $225 million to show outperformance. Business activity seemed solid.

Some investors may be wondering why growth isn't that high despite their strong AI momentum. Well, while news coverage is now mainly focussed on their AI business, you can see in the above table that Cisco in reality is a quite diversified business with solutions spanning security to observability. You can also see that even though networking saw robust growth, areas like services were somewhat of a drag to their performance. Nonetheless, networking is their most important business now and the 21% of growth for Q2 was a notable acceleration from Q1's 15% and so there are indeed signs that AI continues to provide meaningful tailwinds for their business activity.

Is Demand Softening?

When looking at their RPO and ARR results, there may be some concern that demand is softening. In just a second we'll talk about their order stats but as you can see above, Product RPO and Services RPO growth slowed from the previous quarter to 8% and 2%, respectively. For ARR, there was also deterioration in both Product and Services with the latter showing YoY declines. You can also see that these numbers are quite a contrast to the year ago quarter's numbers and so there are signs that momentum for Cisco is weakening.

While RPO and ARR stats are potentially worrying, there are also still signs that their AI business is solid. In Q2, they saw 18% of product order growth which is a speedup from 13% in the previous quarter. For networking orders in particular they saw a 20% jump YoY. This is just a hair lower than the networking revenue growth rate that was reported in the quarter. Lastly, AI infrastructure orders from hyperscalers totalled $2.1 billion in the quarter and that is a massive QoQ increase from $1.3 billion in Q1. Therefore, while there may potentially be some softness in other areas of their business, AI tailwinds seem set to continue in my view.

Memory Costs Are A Risk

As mentioned by a Seeking Alpha article back in February, memory costs are a concern for Cisco's profitability. With higher hardware mix also being a factor, gross margin saw contraction from 68.7% to 67.5% YoY for the company as a whole. Cisco was able to control their expenses in an impressive manner, however.

OPEX dropped by 110 basis points YoY as a percentage of revenues and that indicates an increasingly well run business. That was able to offset most of that gross margin weakness but ultimately operating margin saw some modest contraction.

On the bottom line, there was growth acceleration from the previous quarter and they also edged past EPS expectations. Still, moving forward investors would be wise to keep an eye on their gross margin as it is still to be seen whether they will effectively raise product prices to offset memory cost headwinds.

Full Year Outlook Raised

Cisco provided Q3 revenue guidance that was above consensus and EPS guidance that was in-line. However, let's focus on the annual outlook for a better sense of where things are at in the grand scheme of things. After beating on the top and bottom lines for Q2, Cisco raised their annual guidance.

For revenue, at the new midpoint, they are implying annual growth of around 8%. While that doesn't seem overly exciting, note that growth in 2025 was just 5% and so in fact there are signs that business activity will strengthen in a meaningful manner moving forward.

As for EPS, the new midpoint would represent an approximately 9% increase annually. Since that outpaces the projected top line growth rate slightly, it likely means that strong OPEX control will again offset the potential gross margin weakness. Memory costs are still a risk moving forward but as a whole, profitability is expected to remain resilient.

The Value Of AI

Before moving on to a discussion of some recent events, it is worth mentioning the findings in Cisco's recent State of Industrial AI Report as it shows that AI is providing real value. AI bears question the payoff from investments but Cisco's findings show that the opportunity at hand is large. In a recent press release, it is stated that

The double-blind global study surveyed more than 1,000 operational technology (OT) decision‑makers across 19 countries and 21 industrial sectors. The findings show that AI is now delivering measurable operational benefits in use cases such as process automation, automated quality inspection, predictive maintenance, logistics, and energy forecasting. However, many organizations are increasingly constrained by readiness gaps in networking infrastructure, cybersecurity, and IT/OT operating models as AI shifts into real‑time, production‑grade use in physical environments.

At this point, it seems that Cisco is well positioned to step into these readiness gaps. The company obviously offers networking solutions but note that they also specialize in areas such as security. Therefore, I believe their assessment of the industry shows that their growth runway moving forward is solid.

Recent Opportunities & Risks

Back in early March it was reported that Nvidia (NVDA) and several telecom companies were committing to building the 6G network on AI-native, open, and secure platforms. Cisco is part of this initiative and so this is another reminder that the company's reach is far beyond just data center products. By now, it is becoming increasingly obvious that 6G will be crucial for physical AI and I believe Cisco's expertise in wireless connectivity positions the company well for this wave. Therefore, AI tailwinds seem set to broaden moving forward.

Going back to security solutions again, Cisco is in position to see tailwinds in this area as well. Just days ago, Anthropic announced Project Glasswing. As Brandon Evans of Seeking Alpha writes, this is

a cybersecurity initiative that will be built with the capabilities of its most powerful model, Claude Mythos Preview, with help from the most prolific software and security providers.

One of Anthropic's partners for this initiative is Cisco and so the wide reach of the company's offerings is again paying off for them. Their participation in a direction that may fundamentally change the trajectory of cybersecurity moving forward again shows that tailwinds are broadening.

In addition to the memory cost risks highlighted earlier, the conflict in the Middle East could be a threat to their operations and infrastructure in the region. On April 1st, it was reported that Iran has threatened a list of tech companies. Sure enough, Cisco was included on that target list. While long term effects may be limited, in the near term, the company has likely experienced disruption in the region and that may impact their performance moving forward.

Valuation Still At A Discount

Cisco's forward P/E ratio has been on a relatively steady climb in recent times and the current reading of 19.63 is quite near multiyear highs. However, as of this writing, Seeking Alpha shows that their non-GAAP forward P/E is still at a ~9% discount to the IT sector. I believe this shows that Cisco stock remains attractive now.

In today's article, there were some mixed numbers discussed but as a whole, Cisco's fundamentals and outlook are still robust in my view. For example, RPO and ARR numbers were a bit shaky but strong order growth shows that AI demand remains solid. Furthermore, while gross margin is a risk, strong OPEX control in Q2 and raised full year EPS guidance show that profitability is expected to remain resilient. Lastly, while there are other near term risks like the Middle East war, the company's expertise in wireless connectivity and cybersecurity is paying off as long term opportunities arise.

Cisco's fundamentals aren't perfect and they don't need to be at this stage. AI tailwinds are set to continue in a strong manner and in fact tailwinds are set to broaden. With the forward P/E ratio still at a discount to the sector, I believe Cisco's current stock price offers a favorable risk/reward setup.

Conclusion

Cisco stock has seen just modest gains since my previous buy rating but as stated at the beginning, given the risk-off state in the markets, this resilience should not be complained about. After today's analysis, I have decided to reiterate my buy call. Things like the gross margin weakness and Iran threats have weighed on sentiment a bit but make no mistake, Cisco is well positioned to take advantage of the opportunities at hand. As stated above, tailwinds are set to broaden and with the valuation still being attractive, now isn't a bad time to build a position in this AI leader.

data.title: Camtek: An Enticing HBM Opportunity, But Better Off On The Watchlist For Now
data.description:
Introduction

Camtek Ltd. (CAMT), an Israeli-based entity that produces high-end inspection and metrology systems for wafer manufacturers and OSAT (outsourced semiconductor assembly and test) players, has gotten off to a flier in 2026. We’re only three-and-a-half months into the new year, but CAMT, which has a client base of over 300 customers, has already seen its market-cap expand by almost two-thirds, when other tech-oriented businesses from Israel have barely eked out any gains; meanwhile, note that CAMT has also outperformed its semiconductor cohort by over 4x during the same period!

So, what’s so great about this business, and is the stock worth pursuing after this momentous run?

Why Should CAMT Be On Investors’ Watchlist?

Investors who are scurrying around the markets, looking for underappreciated, high AI quotient (AI-related revenue as a function of total revenue) businesses, may be interested to note that around half of CAMT’s topline comes from the sale of products supporting AI-applications and high performance computing [HPC], with this share only expected to increase going forward. Add to that a smaller but also lucrative non-AI related advanced packaging opportunity, and you have a business with a lion’s share of growth coming in at 30-40% p.a.

Speaking of the AI and HPC chip opportunity, note that these chips are innately quite sophisticated, combining layers of memory and logic, and they will only get more intricate and smaller over time. However, producing batches of pristine “known-good packages” in high-volumes, isn’t going to be particularly straight-forward, and certainly won’t be possible without the aid of elite inspection and metrology infrastructure that can help adroitly weed out defective batches of chips and ramp the overall production yield.

An inspection and metrology specialist like CAMT looks well set to address the opportunities here, more so with the aid of their Hawk platform, which was only launched around 14 months ago. Hawk, which offers a 2x superior throughput to the company’s previous generation of inspection systems-Eagle G5 (both HAWK and Eagle G5 are set to account for 50% of total sales in 2026), can detect defects to the tune of 150nm. Assessing bump heights, which is a key part of the inspection and metrology process, also becomes a lot easier with this system having the capabilities to identify 500m bumps per wafer. Besides bump heights, CAMT’s 3D metrology systems can also ascertain die tilts, wafer bows, coplanarity, etc. something which traditional 2D optical systems are not particularly best positioned for. Leave aside 2D; even within the 3D metrology space, CAMT’s offerings are seen as the “ tool of reference”.

Investors maybe interested to note that HAWK is continuing to see strong traction in the market, with tier 1 IDMs in particular taking a keen interest with repeat orders.

One underappreciated aspect of HAWK accounting for a larger share of the revenue mix, is that it comes at higher ASPs (average selling prices), and this is expected to reflect favorably on CAMT’s gross margin. Combine higher ASP products, with potentially higher sales volume, and you have a wonderful recipe to drive operating leverage and further operating margin progression. Even otherwise, it’s worth considering that this is a business that has largely progressed its operating margins every year (with the exception of FY23) over the last 5 years.

CAMT should also be considered because it has a resilient enough balance sheet, with a net cash position (where cash exceeds debt), and a record high cash and investment position of $668M. Incidentally cash and investments account for the lion’s share of total assets at 53%, bringing some degree of defensiveness to this story. Also consider that CAMT's half a billion worth of debt consists mainly of senior convertible notes that are due only in 2030 and for which the company doesn't have to pay any interest costs.

While the cash position has been boosted by taking on debt, investors should still note that this is a business model that is turning out to be a lot more mature with an increased threshold of free cash flow every year.

As you can see from the image below, for much of its lifetime, CAMT has struggled to generate any meaningful free cash flow. But this narrative shifted from FY20, and since then, we can see an increasing threshold of FCF every year, with CAMT now converting well over one-fourth of every dollar sold into free cash.

Is CAMT Stock Worth Pursuing Now?

Despite the allure of this CAMT business, I don’t feel investors should get too carried away with an investment in the CAMT stock at this juncture, even if it is trading above all its key moving averages on the daily chart.

Here’s why I am less enamored by a fresh long position in CAMT now.

Firstly, forward valuations are hardly ideal. This is a stock that has traditionally been priced at a 5-year forward average of a little over 31x earnings; now, it is priced at a over 50x forward earnings, which also translates to whopping +60% premium over its average.

CAMT’s current premium doesn’t just exist against its own average, but also against other entities that compete with our stock in the same business (albeit the premium isn’t as pronounced). We’ve used a mix of 5 other giant-caps, large-caps, mid-caps and small-caps to make this peer set more well-rounded, and at an average forward P/E multiple of roughly 47x, it appears that this is not a cheap industry to own. However, note that CAMT now is cheaper only than one stock- Teradyne (TER), which also specializes in systems that weed out defective chips, but the medium used here is “automated” testing equipment.

I wouldn’t necessarily be averse to acceping CAMT’s re-rating and paying a premium, if the business was atleast set to deliver decent enough earnings growth, but that isn’t the case.. As you can see from the image below, the incumbents from this industry are set to deliver impressive average annual growth of 120%, skewed no doubt by the small-cap- INTT’s prospects. However, even if we were to take out INTT from the equation, you’re still looking at pretty decent earnings growth of 25%; against that backdrop, CAMT’s expected growth of only around mid-single-digits, is an eye-sore of sorts, more so when you consider what sort of a valuation multiple you’re shedding out (translating to an implied PEG ratio of 9x).

A potential investment in CAMT now is also dampened by the fact that it looks like one of the more overbought stocks within the landscape of Israeli technology business. The current relative strength [RS] ratio of nearly 3x, is almost at record highs, trading at almost a 150% premium over the long-term mean of 1.22x, and could dissuade investors who rotate funds within this universe to shun CAMT.

Finally, investors should also zoom out, and look at CAMT’s long-term charts on the larger time frame (the monthly chart). If you connect the peaks of the price action over the last 5-6 years (the black line), you’ll see that that the stock has never quite been able to break past it. Over the last 3 months we’ve seen CAMT hugging that upward sloping resistance yet again. In addition to that also note that the price is now trading outside the upper bollinger band (a measure of upward overextension as it represents two standard deviations more than the 20-month rolling average).

To conclude, given these overextended signs (both on the standalone and relative strength charts), the presence of a long-standing resistance, a premium valuation, and sub-par forward earnings growth, I don’t believe it is the best time to deploy funds in CAMT, and thus rate the stock as a hold.

data.title: Euroseas: Stronger Coverage, Higher Rates, Same Deep Discount
data.description:
In the past couple of articles I've shared on Euroseas (ESEA), I've been bullish on this quite small containership owner on the grounds that it has continued to trade below a charter-adjusted NAV. Because of that and the fact that the company has been carrying a fleet whose cash flows have been visible far out into the calendar, my thesis has been that there is a safety net and potential for enduring upside.

That was the heart of the thesis in Q2, and it stayed pretty intact in Q3. Today, I am revising the stock after the Q4 report has already been out for a while to support that not only does that very case still stand, but the shape of the story is even cleaner now.

Euroseas closed last year with another very profitable quarter, a dividend hike, deeper forward coverage, and a clean balance sheet, so the fact that the stock remains equally discounted today despite its prolonged rally suggests further gains could very well be sustained. Given the fact that containership leasing rates continue to surge, I believe ESEA stock remains quite compelling today.

Charter Book

Euroseas has been sitting on a strong charter book since the pandemic days, when rates surged and liners were locking up ships for longer durations. It was already well covered when I last looked at the stock. But by Q4, that coverage had gone out even further, to almost 87% for this year and 71% for 2027. And if you look at management's commentary on the following slide in the Q4 presentation, average rates are still moving higher into 2028.

Momentum toward the Q4-end started to pick up, as Euroseas booked Leonidas Z, Gregos, and Terataki on 35- to 37-month charters at $30,000 per day. Then in February, EM Spetses, a 2007-built 1,740 TEU feeder, was extended for 22 to 24 months at $21,500 per day, over $3,000 per day above its existing rate.

With Euroseas' next-12-month cash flow breakeven remaining so manageable at about $12,190 per vessel per day, it's fairly easy to see why the company will keep printing money against charter rates locked in the low-$30,000s on much of the fleet. Rates remain on the rise as well, but I will get to that in a minute.

Fleet Moves

Euroseas' fleet has changed a little since my last article. The company sold the 2005-built Marcos V in October for $50 million, disposing of a 20+-year-old 6,350 TEU vessel. After the sale, the fleet had 15 feeders and 6 intermediates, and the fleet's average age was 13.1 years. Therefore, compared with Q3, the company is a touch smaller on the water today.

The newbuild side is where things get more interesting. At the end of Q4, not a whole lot had changed. Euroseas still had four 4,484 TEU intermediate newbuilds due in 2027 and 2028, and all of them had already been placed on long-term employment. That helps explain why so much of those two years is already booked and why the growth/fleet replacement story looks fairly low-risk at this point.

However, after the quarter, management remained active as they ordered two 2,800 TEU high-reefer containerships for delivery in June and August 2028 for ~$46.35 million each, with options for up to four more. I like that move because, once again, it was quite calculated. Euroseas gets to pursue modern tonnage while maintaining a healthy balance sheet in the face of rising chartering rates.

Rates Stay Hot

I have already mentioned rising rates a couple of times, so here's why current conditions continue to favor Euroseas. Note that freight rates themselves have been more volatile than charter rates. Still, one-year charter rates were near historic highs through Q1 of this year across all classes.

That lines up with the HARPEX Index. Harper Petersen’s April 10 assessments had:

  • 1,700 TEU ships at $17,250/day,
  • 2,500 TEU at $33,000/day ,
  • 4,250 TEU at $44,000/day, and
  • 6,500 TEU at $55,000/day

for 6-to-12-month periods. The broader HARPEX index also sits at a 3+ year high of 2,239, which is obviously great news for Euroseas and the upcoming bookings we will see with respect to the 2026-2027 gaps in the calendar.

The NAV Math Still Works

Back in Q3, I had noted that Euroseas' estimated charter-adjusted NAV was about $84.9 per share. At the time, the stock traded at $61.55, thus at a 30% discount. Given the move up in rates, it makes sense that ESEA’s charter-adjusted vessel value was higher this time, reaching $664.2 million. That put NAV at about $660.9 million, which works out to $93.7 a share.

The stock is trading at $69.15 right now, which implies roughly a 27% discount. That’s narrowed a little from where it was in my mid-December write-up. But with rates having gone up further since February, today’s actual NAV is probably higher too, so the discount may still be closer to 30%+. So yes, the stock has gone up, but it still looks about as cheap as before, and now it also comes with some extra tailwinds like more coverage days and higher rates.

I should mention that there is some risk in the industry on the supply side of the equation, because the overall containership orderbook has climbed to a worrisome ~35% of the fleet in TEU terms. Euroseas acknowledges the possibility of a softer market ahead if Red Sea rerouting fades or freight stays weak. However, allow me to remind you that Euroseas’ exposure is still concentrated in feeders and intermediates. There, supply looks less oppressive than in the giant boxship classes.

Conclusion

Aside from a brief period between early 2024 and mid-2025, when I wrote two neutral takes, I’ve stayed bullish on ESEA since late 2021. It's also fun to note that since my initial November 2021 piece, the stock is up a market-beating 334%.

In more recent quarters, I have held the view that as long as Euroseas keeps extending coverage, harvesting attractive rates, and modernizing the fleet, all while its stock trades far below charter-adjusted NAV, one can take advantage of its rising profits and the potential closing of the valuation gap to make good money on the stock.

Returns continue to compound due to the company winning on the coverage and rates fronts. Still, the valuation gap has yet to close. With rates on the rise set to continue driving strong profits and the NAV gap supporting the case for additional returns while proving a wide margin of safety, I maintain my bullish stance on ESEA.

data.title: New Era Energy & Digital: Still Not Suitable For Investors
data.description:
The last article on New Era Energy & Digital, Inc. (NUAI) mentioned that the company seemed to have a chief existence of raising cash at the current time. Therefore, the latest stock offering that netted the company nearly $100 million should not have come as a surprise. The stock offering itself was for roughly 30 million shares with an overallotment of roughly 4 million more. The prospectus itself used roughly 60 million shares outstanding as the number of shares issued before the offering.

Going Concern Statement

That is quite a bit of dilution for a company with a going concern statement.

Going Concern

Our cash and cash equivalents are not sufficient to fund our planned operations for a period of at least one year from the date these financial statements are issued. Until we can generate substantial revenue and achieve profitability, we will need to raise additional capital to fund our ongoing operations and capital needs. There is no assurance, however, that additional financing will be available when needed or that we will be able to obtain financing on terms acceptable to us. These conditions raise substantial doubt about our ability to continue as a going concern.

This comes from the annual report. To me, that statement above eliminates this idea as an investment consideration for most investors. Consider that a “full stop” and really not go any further. But there is more still.

Litigation

But there is litigation in addition to the going concern statement with the state of New Mexico concerning the company's existing oil & gas business:

Another key idea from the litigation is that this company only recently entered the data center business. The company was in another business, as demonstrated by the litigation above, and that business has yet to be completely closed down.

That makes one wonder exactly how long the data center emphasis will last as a strategy.

As shown above, the company was really formed in fiscal year 2024. More importantly, one has to question why any oil and gas business came along with a data center creation idea. The two are very separate industries. This is especially true since the New Mexico litigation can take time and money (among other resources) away from the proposed business of the company.

SharonAI Interest Acquisition

This particular transaction results in the holders of the SharonAI business making quite a profit at the expense of company shareholders. I do assume that since both companies had a 50% interest, the value of their contributions was about equal.

It turns out that a fair amount of the recent stock offering will pay for the above transaction. I leave it to readers to read the stock prospectus on that part of the deal. That stock offering dilutes current shareholders in the 50% neighborhood.

Very little (relatively speaking) of that share offering is going towards future expenses that help the company achieve its goal of creating data centers (which the last article covered). Instead, much of the stock offering is going towards repaying debt like the convertible note above.

Even that convertible note has a provision for some value to be repaid with shares instead of cash. This makes the next quarterly report a “must-read” for any shareholder or potential investor. The actual balance sheet with a reported cash balance will be extremely important to see, as then we will know the details of this transaction.

No matter what, SharonAI has made quite a profit on this venture that has yet to show any profit at all for the company.

Income Statement

As a general rule, I want a five-year history for a newly public company as constituted before I consider investing. Since this company was really formed in 2024, I would still be watching the action from the sidelines.

As noted above, all the company's revenue comes from the oil & gas business that is under litigation. But even that has lease operating expenses exceeding the revenue. That is hardly helping the cash burn situation for this essentially development-stage company.

If anything, that revenue indicates a lack of focus on the current strategy. It also brings into question the seriousness of the current strategy (compared to a major investor getting out of a tough situation). It will be very interesting to see how the New Mexico litigation unfolds, given the financials shown above.

The impairment expenses of those oil and gas assets are not encouraging either. It would appear that the company will take a sizable loss on acquired assets without having seen material benefits from these assets in its short existence.

Cash Flow Statement

Cash flow indicates a substantial burn rate with a mention of oil and gas.

Respective amounts spent on various expenditures are not given. But again, one has to wonder about the presence of the oil & gas business compared to a stated goal of creating data centers.

Given that much of the cash raised will be spent repaying current debt, any cash raised is likely not to last long unless there is a clear focus on the data center goal, combined with far more detail on a long-term plan, complete with financing.

Credit Facility

Now the company did mention a $290 million credit facility. But rarely is all that money available without “strings attached.” Some conditions are stated in the announcement. However, generally, the actual agreement has more complications than ever fit into an announcement.

Moreover, as to whether or not this gets the company to actual revenue and expenses of a going concern is entirely up for discussion.

For me, there is definitely not enough history for me to even consider such a successful conclusion. I, instead, will get out the popcorn and soda to watch from the sidelines.

Summary

Most new companies “disappear without a trace” within roughly five years. This is covered in just about any finance book that covers new issues. David Dreman touches on this in his “Contrarian Investing” series of books, while Ken Fischer touches on this with his “Super Stocks” series of books. I am sure this gets covered in a lot of other places.

Both authors would tell you this company has about 3 more years to go, and the outlook is not really good. As a result, this stock has to be a very strong sell (as in AVOID big time). There have got to be better choices out there.

Risks

A positive outlook depends upon the satisfactory settlement of the New Mexico litigation, combined with the accomplishment of the data center goals. Currently, that would appear to be a very tall order.

The person taking the job of Chief Operating Officer and President was announced in January 2026. A Chief Financial Officer came a month or so later. There is not a lot of history for the company or the senior officers at this time. Maybe everything will turn out ok in the future despite the short history. But I would say it would be more than reasonable to have doubts about the future given the situation.

The “going concern” statement in the annual report makes this idea a nonstarter for me despite the successful sale of stock. That statement makes the risk level far too high for the average investor. Combined with the payment of debt and the cash burn, I really have a hard time seeing a successful future.

The stock dilution this year signifies a very significant long-term risk in that the company could succeed. But the stock dilution to get there could be substantial enough to eliminate any investment profit.

data.title: What Does The Iran War Mean For Emerging Markets?
data.description:
By Adriaan du Toit, Jingting Deng and Edward Williams

In a volatile period, we believe emerging markets’ stronger fundamentals will shine through the noise.

Conflict in the Middle East—and the resulting energy shock—has raised concerns for emerging markets. Given the breadth of the asset class, we expect implications to vary across countries and issuers, reflecting whether economies are energy exporters or importers, roles in AI supply chains, and supply chain adaptability. Overall, however, our analysis reveals that emerging markets face the current crisis from a position of strength.

Indeed, more credible economic policies, improved governance, lower fixed-income risk premiums and reduced susceptibility to inflation all point to emerging markets’ increasing convergence with developed markets. In addition, emerging countries’ potential growth rates look robust compared with many developed peers.

Yet, in capital markets, a headline-driven, volatile environment is creating greater dispersion across emerging-market countries, sectors and companies. We believe the emerging world’s enormous breadth offers meaningful opportunity to find countries and securities whose valuations have become disconnected from fundamentals.

The US Dollar Remains Central to the Overall Outlook

A weakening US dollar remains central to a positive emerging-market outlook, because emerging countries’ hard-currency debts are mostly denominated in dollars, as are vital commodities.

The initial air strikes on Iran triggered a risk-off market phase, and with it an uptick in the US dollar. But we believe the dollar’s recent weakening trend will resume. The reason? Not only is the currency still expensive relative to history, but structural forces—including concerns about the direction of US policy—continue to drive de-dollarization.

A weaker dollar typically means easier monetary conditions, space for rate cuts in developing countries, and lower risk aversion—all favorable for emerging markets (Display).

Oil Is a Major Driver for Emerging Countries

Higher oil prices were the war’s immediate economic impact. As with most geopolitical events, the market’s initial reaction has been driven by volatility and risk aversion. What ultimately matters for fundamentals, however, is the duration of the conflict rather than the magnitude of the initial price shock. If sustained, continuing higher energy prices are likely to lift headline inflation and weigh on global growth. As a result, financial conditions quickly began to tighten when the conflict began.

Higher energy prices don’t impact every emerging economy through the same channels, but financial markets start by distinguishing between oil importers and oil exporters (Display).

To assess resilience to shocks, it’s also important to assess countries’ respective policy spaces and reserve buffers. Remittances, tourism and proximity to the conflict zone are all important considerations too, in our analysis.

Taking these factors into account, Egypt, Pakistan, Sri Lanka and Kenya appear among the most vulnerable countries, with Ecuador among the best-placed. The larger economies, such as Brazil, South Africa and Mexico, look more resilient. Latin American energy and commodity exporters are also ones to watch.

Technology Contributes to Many Emerging Economies

It’s important to keep in mind other major trends affecting emerging markets—even as the war in Iran draws headlines. Several Asian countries are central to AI supply chains, and many emerging nations globally provide AI-critical commodities, putting them in the lead in the global AI rally (Display).

Beyond semiconductors and memory chips, emerging Asian manufacturers also offer “backdoor,” less-known AI exposures such as power and thermal management and data center components. Not only are these less visible investment opportunities, but they also benefit from higher barriers to entry and longer investment cycles, in our analysis.

Reconfiguration of Supply Chains Makes Security Selection Vital

We expect emerging countries to reconfigure their supply chains to mitigate the impact of the oil shock and its second-order effects.

Accordingly, from a security-selection perspective, it’s vital to identify the winners and losers within the same value chain. We think the big winners will have higher technology standards, offer greater reliability and align geopolitically.

For instance, the disruptions to natural gas supplies have increased global demand for LNG carriers. But competition is limited, as the costs of entering this market are high, standards are technically demanding, and China and Korea dominate the market. Because energy has become a prominent national security concern, some western buyers may feel reluctant to buy from China, making Korean shipyards the winner by default.

In our view, conflict-driven deglobalization need not reduce the opportunity for emerging-market investors. But it will require more selectivity and intensive bottom-up research to identify the companies in the right parts of the value supply chain.

China Remains Central to the Outlook

China’s economy remains crucial for the Asia-Pacific region and for many emerging countries worldwide, and although China’s property market and domestic consumption remain soft, there are encouraging signs for emerging-market trading partners.

Chinese policymakers have recommitted to keeping real GDP growth between 4.5% and 5%. We also expect them to help ease some of the disinflationary impulses in the economy.

We see several structural positives for China’s securities markets: the government’s anti-involution measures to reduce overcapacity; improving corporate governance (evidenced by more dividends and buybacks); improved capital discipline resulting in negative net equity issuance; and China’s strong position in critical technologies and commodities, especially those linked to electrification.

We’re already seeing more attractive opportunities in insurance and financial companies because the shareholder-friendly policy shift has led to stronger balance sheets in these sectors.

Stay Alert to Risks—and Rebounds

Rather than treating emerging markets as a single macro trade, we think investors should consider allocating selectively across regions and countries and tilting tactically between emerging equity and debt. They can also use multiple security selection techniques to focus on companies with resilient earnings, pricing power and strategic relevance.

Periods of heightened volatility have historically created opportunities. Our research indicates that, following periods of high volatility where valuations become disconnected from fundamentals, emerging markets have bounced back more strongly than their developed counterparts. We favor building positions in well-placed emerging countries and companies ahead of a normalization in volatility and sentiment.

The views expressed herein do not constitute research, investment advice or trade recommendations, do not necessarily represent the views of all AB portfolio-management teams and are subject to change over time.

MSCI makes no express or implied warranties or representations, and shall have no liability whatsoever with respect to any MSCI data contained herein. The MSCI data may not be further redistributed or used as a basis for other indices or any securities or financial products. This report is not approved, reviewed or produced by MSCI.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Nintendo: The Upward Game Should Start Soon
data.description:
Five months after my previous coverage, Nintendo Co., Ltd. (NTDOY) has already dropped by about 40% despite my positive view. I can’t blame the market for this sharp and hasty reaction, and I think this is part of NTDOY’s price cycle. Even I have started to become more conservative by downgrading my rating from strong buy to buy after the stock rose by 50%, from $14.7, my very first analysis in December 2024, to $22 in November 2025. And I admit I failed to note the more important aspects of its fundamentals, which could have prompted me to continue my downgrade to hold. Now, NTDOY is at its one-year low. Even so, new buying opportunities have already opened at this level. Valuation is already cheap, while momentum shows tendencies to improve due to overselling. These are all supported by its robust fundamentals and new growth drivers.

Nintendo: Market Appeal Is Still Strong

Nintendo Co. Ltd. has capitalized on its enticing games and unique consoles, which helped it secure a solid customer base for three or four decades. But since the 2000s, it has learned to adapt to new technologies and even took advantage of them to maintain a strong market position and huge following. And from games, merchandise, and royalties, it extended to movies, videos, and smart-device content, showing its continued evolution in the midst of competition in a fast-paced environment. Just when you thought it had already reached its summit, Nintendo proved it could still go higher. This was evident in its most recent performance.

For the three quarters of FY 2026, its net sales reached ¥1.906T, up by 99.3% YoY from ¥956.22B. In Q3 2026 alone, it generated ¥806.318B, which was already close to the three-quarter sales in FY 2025. This proves the strong sales or demand for Switch 2. As of its most recent report, it has already sold a total of 17.37M units of Switch 2, which was an increase of 7M units, or 67.66%, in a single quarter. This was also the highest number of units sold since it only sold 5.82M units in Q1 2026 and 4.54 units in Q2 2026. I think this was impressive considering stubborn inflation and new tariffs. Geographically, the Americas remained the core growth driver as it comprised over 40% of the global sales. Europe came second with 24.7%. This can tell us that Nintendo’s global reach remained vast as customer loyalty strengthened.

To have a better view of its sales, we can make an assumption of its pricing and sales. As we all know, the base selling price of each Switch 2 is ¥49,980. If we multiply it by the total number of units sold for three quarters, it already generated ¥868.16B. But of course, the price I used is for Japanese-language-only models. To be more precise, I assumed that it sold multi-language models in other regions. With that, the estimated global sales of Switch 2 from Q1 to Q3 2026 reached $1.090T.

Likewise, costs and expenses rose dramatically. Their combined increase of 127% completely offset revenue growth. But of course, it is like comparing apples to oranges because NTDOY hasn’t released Switch 2 yet in the comparative period. We also have to account for the impact of stubborn inflation and higher tariffs, which made production and shipment of materials and products costlier. The good thing is that its sales remained high enough to cover all operating expenses. In the end, its operating income remained a double-digit value at 15.76%.

Risks

Stubborn inflation is still a primary consideration for Nintendo. Although it has already slowed down, it remains above the target of many central banks. For instance, US inflation is still above the 2.0% Fed target, which means that it still hasn’t been totally controlled yet. Positive inflation means prices are still increasing, which may affect purchasing power, discretionary spending, and margins of the company.

This may be exacerbated by new tariffs because exporting parts, materials, and products to the US may become more expensive. This may increase its cost pressures and even the selling prices. Another thing to consider is the Middle East tension. Although NTDOY has a well-diversified geographical presence and is not heavily reliant on oil or fuel, it may still experience the negative spillovers. For instance, higher oil prices may raise its fuel expenses. And because energy commodities affect large industries like shipping, logistics, manufacturing, and industrial, the economy as a whole may be affected. With fueled inflation, it may also have to raise wages so employees can cope with inflation. And because this is not an essential good with typically a strict suggested retail price, it may be difficult for it to raise prices. It may also prioritize volumes and customer loyalty since it also has to consider competition with other brands like Sony and Xbox.

Mitigants/Drivers

Despite these potential risks, I believe that NTDOY has several strengths to protect its operations and support a rebound.

One aspect to consider is its solid customer base. In gaming, loyalty and following are not measured or determined the same way we do for typical merchandise or services. It took it decades to maintain millions of loyal gamers to sustain its growth and expansion. Its demand is somewhat elastic since it has substitutes like the gaming brands I’ve mentioned earlier. However, product differentiation is what dictates the demand and loyalty for each player. After all, you cannot play Zelda on PlayStation and Xbox. Many games are part of Nintendo exclusives that cannot be played on these consoles. The same applies to PS and Xbox. In addition, product/game compatibility in Switch and Switch 2 ensures that it will generate revenues if gamers can’t purchase the latter. That is why it was still able to sell 3.25M units of Switch hardware and 108.93M units of Switch software.

The strong popularity of Pokémon is another growth driver for NTDOY. In a recent report, Pokémon Pokopia sales for the Switch 2 have already surpassed 2.2M units just four days after its launch on March 5, 2026. This shows that NTDOY capitalizes not only on its consoles but also on its games. I believe this was effective because Pokémon is well-loved for many reasons. One is its anime franchise. Let’s be honest. I bet no anime lover and gamer doesn’t know anything about characters like Pikachu, Charizard, Snorlax, Meowth, and Squirtle. Since the late 1990s or early 2000s, Pokémon has already captured our hearts. So, it won’t be surprising to see more sales.

The surprising success of The Super Mario Galaxy Movie is another driver. As of yesterday, the movie has already grossed over $600M since its release in theaters. With its strong ticket sales, it is expected to get close to $1B in the next few weeks. This proves its appeal across all generations. Indeed, it is not only for individual gamers but for their family as a whole. This ensures NTDOY's popularity across the globe today.

Another core strength of Nintendo is its robust liquidity. Its cash and cash equivalents comprise nearly half of the total assets, proving that it has ample liquid assets. The amount is also more than enough to cover all liabilities. Additionally, most of its equity is retained earnings, which proves that NTDOY has been generating enough earnings to cover business operations without resorting to borrowings. Hence, NTDOY may sustain its capacity, dividends, and innovation.

Valuation

To assess its valuation, I assessed its price ratios. Using P/B, the stock trades at 0.80x its book value, which can be considered reasonable. It is also lower than the five-year average of 0.93x. If we use this to derive the fair value, the TP, or target price, will be $15.19, or a 17% upside potential. The same applies to the P/S ratio using sales per share and P/CPS using cash per share, as they lead to TPs of $17.25 and $16.35, respectively. The proximity of their TPs proves that NTDOY is already reasonably cheap with some upside at this level.

More interestingly, we can use the forecasted sales of NTDOY to value P/S in a more forward-looking approach. If we convert the forecasted sales of $2.250T, the USD value will be $14.09B. After dividing it by the total shares of 1,164.3M, the sales per share will be $12.10, while the P/S FWD will be 1.07x ($13.00/$12.10). This is still lower than the average of 1.49x. Using this, the P/S FWD TP is slightly higher at $17.99, proving the cheapness of the stock.

I also made updates on its DCF Model. Because the sales forecast remains the same, I also left its FCFF untouched. But I still updated its cash and cash equivalents based on its most recent report. I still set its perpetual growth rate at 2.0% to be in line with my previous coverage. However, there were notable changes in its WACC. I increased its risk premium from 5.0% in my previous coverage to 6.0% to price in more risks. Lastly, my new estimated Beta is 0.28, which means that NTDOY has become much less volatile than in previous coverage, with pricings much more stable than the S&P 500. With all these numbers, I estimated a TP of ¥17,070, or a 105% upside from its current price of ¥8,297. If we translate this upside potential to its USD value, the TP will be $26.70, which is double the current price.

Technicals

NTDOY’s technicals stay weak as its price moves with lower highs and lows. The stock price is also below the 50-day and 200-day SMA, which shows that the current momentum is still weaker than the average in the past 50 and 200 days. The 50-day SMA is still trending downwards, which means that recent market sentiments have been pessimistic. Yet, we can see that the downtrend has flattened a bit. This may suggest that buyers are entering again, while fewer sellers are accepting price bids below the current price.

It is also worth noting the opposite trend of MACD and RSI. Note the decreasing price lows from December 22 to February 25 to April 10. However, the lows of MACD and RSI indicators on the same dates increased continuously. This can tell us that fewer sellers are influencing the market, while the number of buyers increased as the stock became much cheaper. This can be an early bullish divergence signal. More importantly, the stock is almost oversold now, which may prompt buying volume and drive recovery attempts.

Conclusion

Nintendo Co., Ltd. remains a robust company with its solid customer base, supported by its Switch 2 and Pokémon Pokopia. Its robust liquidity and continued innovation help ensure its strong adaptability and competitiveness while sustaining dividends. In addition, its valuation is already cheap after the selloff. Technicals are still weak today, but early bullish divergence calls after the recent overselling may drive some recovery attempts. I’m reiterating my buy rating on this stock.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: ServiceNow: Cheap Enough To Avoid
data.description:
Today, it's finally a green day in the software industry, and stocks like ServiceNow, Inc. (NOW) are up mid-single digits.

Despite the investor excitement in the software space, I am not joining this rally.

Believe it or not, I am not a bear on ServiceNow. Despite the UBS downgrade last week and the fears of AI disruption, I strongly believe the bull case is not broken.

What I think is broken is the lens that the market is using to value this stock. Based on the nearly 10% drop after a beat and raise in Q4 2025, I believe I was right with my earlier view to remain on the sidelines until the storm comes to an end.

As I discuss in this piece, I don't see any signs of a turnaround. In my view, fundamentals alone are unlikely to lift this stock, and I remain disappointed with the timing of the M&A spree, given what's going on in software after the announcement of Anthropic's Mythos.

Therefore, I believe today's pop is merely due to the beta with the software industry. Just look at the 1-day return heatmap of this industry, with pretty much every constituent in the green:

I don't see enough alpha in ServiceNow to warrant an upgrade. Yes, at 20x forward earnings, this is a cheap stock. However, I am not sure that the multiple compression is a compliment to begin with.

Needless to say, I remain on the sidelines.

The M&A Spree

In my view, the timing for this acquisition spree couldn't have been worse, given the pessimism in the software sector due to AI disruption fears.

Inorganic growth is the last thing you want to hear when the market is worried about the organic growth in this sector.

Let's start with Moveworks.

The company announced this acquisition back in March 2025 for $2.85 billion. In plain English, this acquisition was intended to help employees get support and get routine work done inside the company. Think IT tickets for access, finding company information, resetting passwords, or handling HR questions.

Well, it turns out that the acquisition closed on December 15, 2025, just a few weeks after fears of AI disruption started following Anthropic’s release of Claude Opus 4.5, one of the best models back then for coding and agentic work.

Now, given how late in Q4 the acquisition was done, I wasn't surprised to see these comments from CEO William McDermott during the last earnings call (emphasis added):

Overall, Q4 NNACV growth accelerated both quarter-over-quarter and year-over-year in Q4. Our subscription revenue growth was 21%, 19.5% in constant currency, 1.5 points above the high end of our guidance. Contribution from Moveworks was de minimis.

Looking at the guidance for Q1 and FY 2026, management disclosed the Moveworks contribution.

I'm glad they did, as it shows minimal effect on total growth.

For Q1 2026, the subscription revenue guidance was 18.5%-19% in constant currency. Well, that guidance includes only 100 basis points from Moveworks. In fact, the FY 2026 subscription revenue guidance (19.5%-20% in cc) is also assuming only 100 bps from Moveworks.

The problem that I see comes from the other two acquisitions announced at the end of last year.

Let me start with the main one: Armis.

It was announced back in December last year for $7.75B in cash. It appears that management expects to complete this acquisition in the second half of 2026. Therefore, once closed, the company will have to reassess organic vs. inorganic growth. I see two main issues.

First, given the size of this acquisition (2.7x the value of the Moveworks acquisition), it is likely that its contribution to growth will be significantly higher than the 100 bps of Moveworks. Therefore, the fears of inorganic growth are likely to resume in H2.

Second, Armis is a cybersecurity company. You may already know where the sentiment in this industry is after Anthropic's announcement of Mythos. Otherwise, take a look at the chart below, showing the performance of BUG, a global ETF on cybersecurity.

If that wasn't enough, ServiceNow announced back in December last year a third acquisition. This is a smaller acquisition of a little over $1B for a company that offers an identity security platform (Veza). This acquisition closed in March this year, so we may find out the contribution to growth as soon as the Q1 earnings call. At least, that's what I would expect, given how transparent management was with Moveworks.

ServiceNow Bull Case Still Intact

As I suggested in my last article, fundamentals (or seat growth) aren't deteriorating. Aside from the M&A spree that I discussed above, it's difficult to find any other solid argument against the bull case.

As seen below, the Street expects revenue in the 20s percent for the next 4 quarters.

Despite the recent analyst downgrades, the consensus for earnings has barely changed. In other words, the Street believes the AI disruption fears are just that: fears.

Speaking of downgrades, the stock was down almost 7% last Friday after UBS downgraded ServiceNow to Neutral from Buy and cut its price target to $100 from $170. The same analyst lowered its exit-2026 cRPO growth estimate to 16% constant currency from 20%.

For reference, management guided a cRPO of 20% (cc) for Q1 2026. As I said earlier, the Q1 cRPO guide includes about 100 basis points from Moveworks. So, the organic Q1 growth was about 19%.

Other price target cuts this month included Stifel ($135 from $180), citing a weak U.S. federal spending environment, and BTIG ($185 from $200). Interestingly, analysts here on Seeking Alpha feel quite bullish about this stock, as noted by the recent articles. In fact, as I point out below (red arrow), there was a clear inflection point between hold and buy ratings on this stock, right when the AI disruption fears began.

Are these analysts right?

Give it time, and I believe the answer may be yes. However, I don't see why I should park my capital in a stock that is constantly pressured by headlines and Wall Street analyst downgrades.

This external pressure is compressing a multiple that already looks quite attractive. As I'm writing, the stock is trading at just 20x next year's earnings. When compared to the last 12-month P/E of 50x, it seems like a bargain, in my view.

Looking Ahead

I reiterate my hold on ServiceNow, as I am not convinced that an attractive valuation multiple is enough to turn around the sentiment surrounding this stock.

As seen below, the stock broke a key long-term trend last week, as the fears of Anthropic's Mythos model permeated the industry.

The next technical support seems to be $70. That would be another double-digit drop from today's level. Given the M&A spree and the recent analyst downgrades, I don't see why strong fundamentals in Q1 will turn around this trend.
