Fundamentals certainly didn't help in Q4, after the company beat the analysts' expectations for subscription revenue in 2026 ($15.53B-$15.57B vs. the Street's estimate at $15.21B).

Overall, I am staying on the sidelines on this one.

data.title: Intel Could Be The Next Micron
data.description:
Thesis Summary

A couple of weeks ago, I wrote about how Micron Technology, Inc. (MU) could be the new Intel Corporation (INTC), comparing the similarities between these two back in the 1990s, when Intel dominated hardware.

Today, I present yet another controversial idea: Could Intel be the next Micron?

While Intel is seen by many as a bloated tech company that has missed the AI revolution, the stock is now making new highs and leading the semiconductors.

Micron’s story is all about capturing the memory bottleneck. But where’s the next bottleneck?

I argue below that Intel could re-emerge as the key player of a different, and potentially more strategic, bottleneck.

The market is slowly waking up to this possibility, but it’s not too late to invest in Intel today.

How Bottlenecks Shift In Every Cycle

Back in the 1990s, as I talked about in my Micron article, CPUs were the bottleneck. It was the dawn of the personal computer, and that was the way to access the internet.

Fast forward to the 2010s, and everyone has a computer. Now it’s all about software and cloud infrastructure. We saw big gains for cloud providers and also software companies with the right talent.

Now, AI has changed everything once again. Software has become commoditised, and will likely never be seen in the same light by the market.

But the era of AI created a shortage in GPUs, which originally favoured NVIDIA Corporation (NVDA).

As AI clusters scaled and we moved from training to inference, memory bandwidth became the next constraint, making Micron one of the hottest stocks in the market.

We’ve also seen big rallies in photonics and connectivity stocks recently

Yes, there will be shortages of all these components for the next two years, but that’s already priced in. We need to look at what comes next.

Intel Today: Broken Or Misunderstood?

This is a question I myself have grappled with.

In my last Intel article, I discussed all the problems with Intel, giving it a Sell rating. But the stock is up 30% since then, so clearly I was wrong.

Intel has made a lot of mistakes over the years. It let Advanced Micro Devices, Inc. (AMD) take its share in CPUs. It missed the mobile phone revolution, and it became a tech giant too set in its ways.

But perhaps, finally, Intel has woken up.

Intel is now one of the only Western companies investing in semiconductors, at least at a meaningful scale.

Intel is undergoing the painful and expensive process of building fabs, and expanding capacity. However, in doing so, it is positioning itself as a foundry, not just for the US, but also for external customers.

This way takes some time to play out, but by the time it's obvious, like Micron today, the money will be made.

Manufacturing is where the next bottleneck lies.

The Bottleneck Intel Could Control

Already, there’s clear evidence that AI demand is overwhelming production. Reportedly, Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company Limited (TSM) is reaching its limits in terms of capacity.

Meanwhile, money is pouring into the industry, with Japan reportedly investing $4B to support local chipmakers.

Chip production and certain particular areas around the process are the next obvious, yet not so obvious, bottleneck.

Take advanced packaging, for example/

As AI systems scale, more components are put into a single rack or chip.

These integrated systems combine multiple dies, memory stacks, and interconnects, and this dramatically increases complexity.

Advanced packaging has specifically been acknowledged recently as a potential bottleneck.

If Intel can execute here, through technologies like Foveros and EMIB, it will position itself at a critical junction of the AI stack.

It’s no good having all the pieces if you can’t put them together, and that’s an area in which Intel could actually shine.

And of course, let’s not forget the geopolitical dimension here.

AI has the potential to control everything in the economy, including critical infrastructure, utilities, and defense systems.

As such, it’s not just enough to have the technology; you have to be able to produce it.

That’s why governments and corporations alike are looking for alternatives to TSM, and Intel’s push into foundry services aligns directly with this shift.

Valuation

Even though Intel has had a good run, it is not being priced as a future bottleneck.

Intel is being priced like a turnaround story, but a mild one at that. EPS is expected to slowly move from negative to positive territory, with revenue growing in the single digits over the next couple of years.

I think this misses the bigger point.

If Intel is going to sit at the forefront of a new bottleneck, then margins could have huge upside.

Look at TSM, for example, which has increased its gross margin to over 60%.

This means Intel has room to double margins from here, and EPS could easily surprise to the upside as this happens.

I'm talking about a transformation equivalent to that of Micron, which went from tight margins to over 60% margins and more than 6x earnings in a year.

No one saw it coming, though now it seems obvious, and Intel now has the potential to do the same.

Risks

The most obvious risk is that Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company will continue to dominate the market.

The truth is, it hasn’t really been challenged before, but it’s hard to see Intel displacing TSM overnight.

Intel has some guarantee of customers, since the US has an interest in having its own production, but it ultimately has to attract external customers to its foundry business to create real upside.

Of course, Intel is spending heavily, and if returns do not materialise, this will continue to destroy shareholder value rather than create it.

Lastly, with a lot of other countries investing in fab capacity, perhaps the bottleneck will be addressed before Intel can really reap the benefits.

Final Thoughts

Ultimately, I think Intel has a lot of potential upside here, while the downside is limited. Intel is addressing perhaps the most important part of the AI supply chain and trying to create a real alternative.

If it succeeds, we’re looking at a completely transformed business, with huge earnings potential.
